title: Pain-free patients stop coming. AI brings them back.
excerpt: Chiropractic patients are the most politely lost clients in healthcare. They get better, they stop coming, and six weeks later they’re Googling ‘chiropractor near me’ — and picking a different one. I mapped the drop-off pattern and the reactivation timing that actually works. The 6-week window is the whole thing.

title: Moving companies bleed jobs between booking and moving day.
excerpt: Moving companies lose booked jobs to silence — no communication in the weeks before the move, and customers quietly cancel. Here's the two-gap problem and how AI closes it.

title: Cleaning clients don't cancel. They drift — and an AI catches it.
excerpt: Residential cleaning companies lose recurring clients silently — no cancellation, no complaint. Here's how to spot the drift before it becomes churn, and automate the fix.

title: Plumbers miss emergency calls while they're on jobs. An AI doesn't.
excerpt: Plumbing shops miss their best leads when they're on jobs — and the caller books a competitor within 90 minutes. Here's the AI workflow that closes that gap.

title: Painters quote 30 jobs a month and close 10. The follow-up gap explains the other 20.
excerpt: Painting contractors have a follow-up problem, not a pricing problem. Most lost bids go to whoever responded first. Here's a 3-message system that closes the gap.

title: Gym members ghost before they cancel. Here's the AI that catches them first.
excerpt: Gym members show behavioral signals weeks before they cancel. Check-in drops, class booking stops — the data is there. Here's how AI turns that signal into a retention system.

title: 40% of vet patients are overdue for a visit. Here's the AI that finds them.
excerpt: Veterinary clinics have rich patient data on overdue and at-risk animals — and rarely act on it. Here's what an AI-driven outreach system looks like for a small animal clinic.

title: Most restaurants ignore their guest list. AI doesn't.
excerpt: Restaurants collect thousands of guest contacts through reservations, loyalty apps, and POS systems — then do nothing with them. Here's what an AI-driven win-back system actually looks like.

title: Landscaping companies lose their best accounts in October. An AI sees it coming.
excerpt: Landscaping companies lose recurring clients in the off-season — not from bad work, but from silence. Three messages over four months fix most of it.

title: Auto repair shops ghost customers. An AI follow-up agent doesn't.
excerpt: Auto repair shops lose repeat customers not from bad work — from silence. No update calls, no follow-up, no system. Here's the workflow that fixes it.

excerpt: HVAC contractors lose leads every peak season — not because they're bad at their jobs, but because their phone rings hardest when they can't answer it. Here's the AI setup I'd build on day one.

excerpt: Med spas lose clients to silence — missed follow-ups, lapsed memberships, ghost leads. Here's a practical OpenClaw setup that fixes the gaps without replacing your front desk.

title: Photography studios ghost their best leads. An AI doesn't.
excerpt: A wedding inquiry lands in the studio inbox at 9pm on a Saturday. The photographer is shooting a reception. By Monday morning when they finally sit down to respond, the couple has already booked someone else. I mapped where photography studios lose their best bookings — and what a properly timed AI response does to that conversion rate.

title: Electricians miss emergency calls on the job. An AI doesn't.
excerpt: A homeowner's breaker trips at 9am. She calls four electricians. The first one back wins the job — and probably the next three calls from that house. I mapped where those emergency calls land during a typical electrician's day. It's mostly voicemail, and the lead math is uncomfortable.

title: The permit silence that costs electricians their best referrals
excerpt: An electrician finishes the rough-in, pulls the permit, and goes quiet for two weeks while the inspection process grinds along. The homeowner starts wondering if something went wrong. By the time the job is complete, the experience felt stressful — even though the work was excellent. I mapped exactly where electrical contractors lose referrals they should own. It's almost never the wiring.
